authoringProvenance.author: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Pro (candidate authoring; not a human reviewer)

items.prompt: Select all statements that are valid for integers modulo a positive modulus m.
constraints: Congruence a ≡ b (mod m) means m divides a-b.
All arithmetic statements are mathematical, not language-specific % behavior.

options.text: If a ≡ b (mod m), then a+c ≡ b+c (mod m).
If a ≡ b (mod m) and c ≡ d (mod m), then ac ≡ bd (mod m).
If ka ≡ kb (mod m), then a ≡ b (mod m) for every integer k.
Two residues are congruent exactly when their floating-point decimal representations compare equal.

feedback.reason: Congruence is preserved by addition and multiplication, while cancellation needs an invertibility condition.
feedback.details: Because m divides a-b, it also divides (a+c)-(b+c). If m divides both a-b and c-d, expanding ac-bd=a(c-d)+d(a-b) shows multiplication preserves congruence. Cancellation is different: from k(a-b) divisible by m, a-b need not be divisible by m when k shares a factor with m. Floating decimal equality is neither the definition nor a reliable exact test.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary_cancellation: Cancellation is valid only when k is invertible modulo m, equivalently gcd(k,m)=1 in this setting.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.decimal_equality: Congruence is an exact divisibility relation; rounded decimal representations can agree or differ without determining it.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.addition_preserves: Adding the same c does not change the difference a-b, so the modulus still divides it.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.multiplication_preserves: The product difference can be written as a sum of multiples of the original congruent differences.

items.prompt: A fixed-width signed-integer language evaluates -17 % 5 as -2. For positive representable m, which implementation converts any signed integer x to the canonical residue in [0,m) without requiring an intermediate larger than m?
constraints: m is positive and representable in the working type.
The remainder r=x%m is evaluated without division overflow under the language contract.

options.text: Set r=x % m; return r < 0 ? r + m : r.
Return ((x % m) + m) % m in the same fixed-width signed type.
Return x % m.
Return abs(x % m).

feedback.reason: Conditionally adding m only to a negative remainder selects [0,m) while keeping the corrected value below m.
feedback.details: With truncating remainder, r lies between -(m-1) and m-1. If r is negative, r+m lies between 1 and m-1 and is representable whenever m is; otherwise r is already canonical. The familiar unconditional expression ((x%m)+m)%m is mathematically correct but can form a value near 2m in a bounded signed type, so it needs an additional width precondition. Absolute value changes the residue class, for example mapping -1 to 1 instead of m-1.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.unconditional_double_mod: This formula is algebraically valid, but in the stated fixed-width domain the unconditional r+m can overflow when r is nonnegative and m is near the maximum value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_percent: The prompt explicitly states that % can return a negative value, so the result is not guaranteed to be in [0,m).
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.absolute_remainder: Absolute value changes the residue class: for example, -1 mod 5 is 4, not 1.

items.prompt: Residues a and b are already in [0,m), m>0, and a-b+m fits the chosen integer type. Which expression returns the canonical residue of a-b?
constraints: a and b are canonical residues.

options.text: (a - b + m) % m
a - b
abs(a - b)
(a - b) / m

feedback.reason: For canonical inputs, a-b lies in (-(m-1),m-1), so adding one m is sufficient before the final reduction.
feedback.details: The input bounds prove that a-b+m is between 1 and 2m-1, except the equal case gives m; reducing by m therefore yields exactly the canonical residue. This one-addition form depends on the stated normalization and width preconditions. For arbitrary unnormalized values, use the general double-mod normalization instead. Absolute difference discards direction around the residue cycle.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.subtract_only: When a<b, the result is negative and is not a canonical residue.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.absolute_difference: Absolute value changes the congruence class and loses whether the modular displacement was forward or backward.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.divide_by_modulus: Integer division computes a quotient, not the residue class of the difference.

items.prompt: Solve the congruence 6x ≡ 6 (mod 12). Which conclusion is correct?
constraints: Answers should be stated as residue classes.

options.text: x ≡ 1 (mod 2), so the solutions modulo 12 are 1,3,5,7,9,11.
Cancel 6 and conclude x ≡ 1 (mod 12).
Every residue modulo 12 is a solution because both sides contain 6.
Compute 6/12 as 0.5 and use floating equality to choose x.

feedback.reason: The common factor 6 is not invertible modulo 12; dividing the entire congruence reduces both coefficient and modulus to x ≡ 1 (mod 2).
feedback.details: The congruence means 12 divides 6(x-1), which is equivalent to 2 divides x-1. Direct cancellation modulo 12 would be legal only if 6 had a multiplicative inverse modulo 12, but gcd(6,12)=6. More generally, ax≡b mod m is solvable only when gcd(a,m) divides b, and dividing by that gcd also divides the modulus. Ordinary or floating division is not a substitute for this divisibility reasoning.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.x_one_mod_12: Cancelling 6 without reducing the modulus discards five valid odd residue classes because 6 has no inverse modulo 12.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_x: Even x make 6x congruent to 0 modulo 12, not 6.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.floating_division: The problem is exact integer divisibility; the decimal 0.5 does not encode the residue classes.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly describes the modulus boundary for canonical integer residues?

options.text: m must be positive; for m=1 every integer has canonical residue 0, while m=0 is undefined.
m=0 is valid and leaves every integer unchanged.
A negative modulus automatically defines the same canonical interval [0,m).
Modulo 1 has two canonical residues, 0 and 1.

feedback.reason: The interval [0,m) is meaningful only for positive m; modulus one collapses all integers to the single residue zero.
feedback.details: Modulo m is based on divisibility by m, but a canonical nonnegative representative convention needs m>0. Division or remainder by zero is undefined. A negative modulus can be converted to a positive magnitude under a separately stated convention, but [0,m) is not a valid increasing interval when m<0. The m=1 case is a useful degenerate boundary because every difference is divisible by 1.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero_allowed: Remainder by zero is undefined and no finite residue cycle exists.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.negative_same: The stated canonical interval assumes a positive endpoint; negative-modulus behavior must be normalized by an explicit API contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_has_two_residues: The interval [0,1) contains only 0, and 1 is congruent to 0 modulo 1.

items.prompt: A cursor is at index 2 in a cycle of length 7 and moves by -5. Which canonical index is reached?
constraints: The language remainder may be negative.

options.text: -3, because 2-5=-3 and one % operation is always canonical.
3, because absolute value removes the negative sign.
0, because a negative move clamps to the start of the cycle.

feedback.reason: Cyclic movement preserves the residue class of the signed displacement and then selects its representative in [0,7).
feedback.details: The raw sum is -3, and -3 differs from 4 by one full cycle of 7, so they represent the same position. Clamping or taking absolute value changes the cyclic operation. This example transfers directly to ring buffers, weekday arithmetic, and repeated rotations. The normalization formula is part of the state invariant, not a display-only correction.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.index_minus_3: A negative remainder may represent the right class but violates the canonical index range required by the data structure.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.index_3: Absolute value maps -3 to a different residue class; the correct class is 4 modulo 7.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.index_0: Cycles wrap rather than clamp unless the problem explicitly defines saturation.

items.prompt: Signed 64-bit a and b may be very large, m is positive, and a*b may overflow. Which plan preserves (a*b) mod m?
constraints: A sufficiently wide product type may or may not be available.

options.text: Normalize a and b modulo m, then use either a sufficiently wide exact product or a proven add-and-double modular multiplication.
Multiply in signed 64-bit and apply %m to the wrapped result.
Compute (a/m)*(b/m) and use the fractional parts to reconstruct the residue.
Use double multiplication and fmod because m is smaller than the inputs.

feedback.reason: Modular reduction can bound operands, but the remaining multiplication still needs an implementation whose intermediates are proven safe.
feedback.details: The congruence rule permits replacing a and b by canonical residues before multiplication. If even the reduced product can exceed the machine type, widening or add-and-double is required. Overflow followed by % computes the residue of a different wrapped integer. Dividing by m loses the remainders that determine the product residue, and floating fmod is not exact for arbitrary 64-bit values.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.overflow_then_reduce: Modulo cannot undo an overflow that changed the product before reduction.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.divide_first: Quotients alone discard the residue information needed to compute the product class.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.double_fmod: Double cannot represent every 64-bit integer or their exact product.

items.prompt: An iterative modular exponentiation routine processes a positive exponent e by repeated squaring, reducing after every multiplication. Classify its time and extra-space costs in terms of e.
constraints: Each multiplication and reduction is O(1) under the fixed-width model.
The routine stores a constant number of residues.

feedback.reason: Repeated squaring consumes one binary digit of the exponent per iteration and keeps only constant state.
feedback.details: At each step, the algorithm conditionally multiplies the result by the current base, squares the base, and halves the exponent. Halving reaches zero after O(log e) iterations. The iterative state consists of the result residue, base residue, exponent, and modulus, so extra space is O(1). This cost statement assumes fixed-width safe modular multiplication; arbitrary-precision multiplication would add its own bit complexity.
